title: Building a no-dig lasagne bed with sheet mulching
subtitle: Smothering weeds and building soil with layered cardboard, compost, and mulch
excerpt: Sheet mulching (also called lasagne gardening) suppresses weeds, retains moisture, and improves soil structure without digging. A base layer of cardboard smothers existing vegetation; subsequent layers of organic material build up the growing medium above. The bed is ready to plant into the same season on most sites.
sourceNotes: Charles Dowding: No Dig Gardening (2022), the standard practical reference on no-dig methods. Patricia Lanza: Lasagna Gardening (1998) on layered bed building. RHS guidance on weed suppression and no-dig techniques.

glossaryTerms.definition: A growing method where the soil is not turned or cultivated. Compost and organic matter are applied as surface layers; worms and soil life incorporate them over time.
A fast-growing crop dug or cut into the soil to improve organic matter content and nitrogen. In a no-dig system, cut and leave rather than dig in.

content.text: A sheet-mulched bed can be built on compacted clay subsoil, rough grass, or a weed-infested patch and be ready to grow vegetables within the same season. The cardboard base layer smothers grass and annual weeds within 6 to 8 weeks. Persistent perennial weeds (bindweed, horsetail) are suppressed for one season but may re-emerge from roots below the cardboard; these sites benefit from a second mulch layer in year two.

Cardboard: corrugated cardboard boxes with all tape removed. A single layer overlapping by at least 150 mm at all joints. Cardboard decomposes in 3 to 6 months in warm soil, by which point roots have penetrated through it.
Organic material layers: compost, aged manure, leaf mould,
cuttings, grass clippings with cardboard layers, straw. A finished bed height of 150 to 300 mm above the cardboard provides enough growing medium for most annual vegetables.
Topping mulch: wood chip, straw, or a final layer of mature compost. The top layer suppresses weed germination and retains moisture.
Mow or cut down existing vegetation as low as possible. Leave the cuttings in place as a nitrogen layer beneath the cardboard.
Soak the cardboard thoroughly. Dry cardboard sheds water and decomposes slowly; wet cardboard collapses and starts decomposing immediately.
Lay the cardboard with 150 to 200 mm overlaps at all joins and around all edges. Tuck the edges under at the bed margins to prevent weed creep from the sides.
Apply the first compost layer, 100 mm deep. Then a thin layer of straw or aged wood chip. Then more compost. Repeat to achieve 150 to 300 mm total.
Water the finished bed and allow to settle for at least 2 to 3 weeks before planting. Transplants do well immediately; direct sowing is more reliable after the layers have begun to knit together.
What to expect in year one

bed built in autumn on rough grass is ready for planting by spring. The cardboard has mostly decomposed; worms from the existing soil have moved into the compost layers above. Tomatoes, squash, courgettes, and potatoes planted through the compost layer in early summer perform on par with dug beds in most UK garden soils. By year two, the soil structure below the original cardboard layer is noticeably improved by worm activity and organic matter infiltration.
